title: Thinking in Loops: Reasoning Models
subtitle: How AI learned to slow down and actually think
theme: Move learners from "AI is a fast guesser" to "AI can pause, work things out, check itself, and try again" — and feel genuine wonder that slowing down made machines smarter.
learningObjectives: Explain the difference between a quick, blurted answer and a worked-out, step-by-step one
Describe chain of thought in plain words — an AI showing its work like a student on a math test
Understand the try-check-retry loop and why looping makes AI more reliable on hard problems
Recognize what reasoning models are and where you meet them in 2026
Use a simple everyday analogy to explain reasoning AI to a friend who knows nothing about it
storyHook: Imagine asking a friend a tricky riddle. One friend shouts the first thing that pops into their head — sometimes right, often wildly wrong. Another friend goes quiet, mutters to themselves, scribbles a bit, then gives you a careful answer that actually holds up. For years, AI was the first friend: fast and confident, but easily tripped up. Today's story is about how AI learned to become the second friend — the one who slows down and thinks it through.

slides.title: Welcome Back: The Friend Who Slows Down
slides.body:
Yesterday, on Day 15, we met AI **agents** — assistants that don't just answer, they go off and *do* things, step by step, like an intern running errands for you. Today we look at something happening one layer deeper: what's going on *inside the AI's head* while it works.

Here's the puzzle we're going to solve together. For most of AI's life, it answered instantly — you typed, it replied in a heartbeat. Fast felt smart. But fast also meant it sometimes blurted out confident nonsense, especially on hard problems like tricky math or multi-step logic.

Then something surprising happened. Researchers discovered that if you let the AI *slow down* — to mumble to itself, work through the steps, check its own answer, and try again — it got dramatically better. Slower became smarter.

Think of it like the difference between guessing on a test versus actually working the problem out on scratch paper. Today's lesson is the story of how AI got its scratch paper. By the end, you'll be able to explain "reasoning models" to anyone at a dinner table. Let's go.
slides.speakerNotes: Open by linking back to Day 15 agents. Emphasize the emotional hook: slowing down made machines smarter, which feels counterintuitive.
slides.marketConnection: In 2026, when you pick a 'thinking' mode in an AI app and watch it pause before answering, you're seeing exactly this idea in action.

slides.title: The Blurt vs. The Worked-Out Answer
slides.body:
Picture two students taking a math quiz. The first one reads the question and instantly writes down a number — no working, just a guess that *feels* right. The second one pauses, writes "okay, first this, then that," works line by line, and arrives at an answer.

For most of its life, AI was the first student. It learned to predict the most likely next word incredibly fast, so it answered in one smooth rush. For easy questions — "What's the capital of France?" — that's perfect. The blurt is correct.

But hand it a problem with several steps, like a logic puzzle or a word problem with a twist, and the fast blurt often goes wrong. Not because the AI is dumb, but because it never *paused* to lay out the steps. It tried to leap straight to the finish line.

The big realization was simple and human: hard problems need working-out. A **step-by-step answer** just means the AI spells out each stage instead of jumping to the end. That single shift — from blurt to working-out — is the seed of everything in today's lesson.
slides.speakerNotes: Use the two-students image throughout the day; it's the spine. Stress that the AI isn't 'dumber' when it blurts — it just skipped the steps.
slides.marketConnection: Many 2026 chat apps now offer a fast mode and a 'think harder' mode — the same blurt-versus-working-out choice, handed to you as a button.

slides.title: Chain of Thought: Showing Its Work
slides.body:
Here's where it gets lovely. Researchers found that if you simply ask the AI to "think step by step," it would start writing out its reasoning — and suddenly get far more answers right.

This is called **chain of thought**: the AI writes out its middle steps, like a student showing their work in the margin instead of only the final answer. "First I'll figure out how many apples there are, then subtract the ones eaten, then..."

Why does writing it out help? Think about doing a big multiplication purely in your head versus on paper. On paper, each step holds the result so you don't lose track. The AI's written steps work the same way — each line becomes a stepping stone the next line can stand on. It's giving itself scratch paper.

The magic isn't that the AI got a new brain. It's that *laying the thinking out in the open* lets it catch its own slips and build toward the right answer instead of gambling on one leap. Showing its work doesn't just help us trust it — it genuinely helps the AI think better.
slides.speakerNotes: Define chain of thought once, clearly, then reuse the phrase. The 'scratch paper' analogy is the keeper.
slides.marketConnection: In 2026 tools, that little expandable 'thinking...' or 'reasoning' panel you can click open is the AI's chain of thought made visible.

slides.title: Try, Check, Retry: The Reasoning Loop
slides.body:
Showing its work was step one. The next leap was teaching AI to **loop**: try an approach, check whether it actually makes sense, and if not, back up and try a different way.

Think about how *you* solve a jigsaw puzzle. You pick up a piece, try it in a gap, see it doesn't fit, set it aside, grab another. You don't expect the first piece to be right — you expect to test and adjust. That loop of try-check-retry is exactly what reasoning AI learned to do with ideas instead of puzzle pieces.

So the AI might work out an answer, then pause and ask itself, "Wait, does that hold up? Let me re-check that middle step." If it spots a mistake, it doesn't just plow ahead — it reconsiders and reworks.

This is the heart of a **reasoning loop**: thinking is no longer a single forward sprint, but a circle of attempt, review, and improve. It's slower, yes. But just like a careful builder who measures twice and cuts once, the extra care pays off in answers that are far more likely to be right.
slides.speakerNotes: The jigsaw analogy lands the 'looping' concept. Emphasize: the AI now treats its first try as a draft, not a final.
slides.marketConnection: When a 2026 coding assistant writes code, runs it, sees an error, and fixes itself before showing you — that's the try-check-retry loop running live.

slides.title: Why Looping Makes AI Far Smarter at Hard Things
slides.body:
Let me tell you about Maya, a baker entering a contest. The first time, she rushes — mixes everything at once, shoves it in the oven, and pulls out a flat, sad cake. Confident and fast, but wrong.

The second time, she slows down. She measures, tastes the batter, notices it's too sweet, adjusts. She checks the oven halfway, sees it browning too fast, lowers the heat. Same baker, same recipe — but the looping of *try, taste, fix* produces a winner.

Reasoning AI got better in exactly this way. On simple questions, the extra looping barely matters — the blurt was already fine. But on genuinely hard problems — tangled math, tricky logic, multi-step planning — the difference is night and day. Giving the AI room to think, check, and redo turned shaky guesses into dependable answers.

There's a beautiful trade here: it costs more time and effort for the AI to think in loops. So the smart move is to use quick mode for easy stuff and the slow, looping mode when the problem actually deserves it — just like you wouldn't measure twice to butter toast, but you would to build a shelf.
slides.speakerNotes: Maya's cake is the emotional centerpiece. End on the trade-off: looping is worth it for hard problems, overkill for easy ones.
slides.marketConnection: This is why 2026 AI products charge more for 'reasoning' answers and save them for hard tasks like research, analysis, and complex coding.

slides.title: The Reasoning Models of 2026
slides.body:
By 2026, this idea has its own family name: **reasoning models** — AIs specifically built to slow down, work step by step, and check themselves before answering.

You'll notice them in everyday tools. Often there's a toggle: a quick mode for casual questions, and a "think harder" or "reasoning" mode for the tough ones. Flip the second one on and you'll often see the AI visibly pause, sometimes showing a stream of its own thinking, before it replies.

Where do they shine? Anywhere the path to the answer has several steps: solving a knotty math or science problem, debugging code, planning a complex trip, weighing the pros and cons of a big decision, or reading a long document and reasoning carefully across it.

The catch, as we saw with Maya, is that thinking takes time and effort, so reasoning answers can be slower and a bit costlier. That's the deal you're making: patience in exchange for reliability. The skill in 2026 isn't just *using* AI — it's knowing *when* to ask it to think hard and when a quick answer will do just fine.
slides.speakerNotes: Keep brand-neutral; describe the category, not specific products. Reinforce the practical when-to-use-it judgment.
slides.marketConnection: Across major 2026 AI assistants, a 'reasoning' or 'extended thinking' option is now standard — and people are learning to reach for it on hard tasks.

slides.title: The Detective Who Talks to Themselves
slides.body:
Here's the analogy to carry home. Picture a detective at a crime scene. A sloppy detective glances around and names a suspect on the spot — fast, dramatic, often wrong. A great detective walks the room slowly, muttering aloud: "If the window was locked from inside, then the intruder couldn't have come this way... so let me reconsider."

That muttering-aloud is the detective's chain of thought. The reconsidering — "let me back up, that doesn't fit" — is the reasoning loop. And the patience to do all this before announcing a conclusion is the whole spirit of a reasoning model.

Notice the detective isn't smarter than the sloppy one in raw brainpower. They just refuse to leap. They lay out the steps, test each one against the evidence, and circle back when something doesn't add up.

That's the gentle truth at the center of today: the breakthrough wasn't a bigger brain. It was *permission to slow down*. We taught machines the very habit good thinkers have always used — work it out, check it, and don't be afraid to start the step over.
slides.speakerNotes: The detective ties chain of thought + loop + patience into one memorable image. Land the closing line: the breakthrough was permission to slow down.
slides.marketConnection: This 'think out loud, then double-check' style is now how 2026 AI tackles research questions, legal reading, and tough customer problems.

slides.title: Recap: Slowing Down to Get Smarter
slides.body:
Take a breath — you just learned how AI grew up from a fast guesser into a careful thinker.

Here's the whole story in one arc. We started with two students: one who **blurts** an instant answer, one who **works it out** step by step. For hard problems, the worker wins.

Then came **chain of thought** — the AI showing its work on scratch paper, which helped it think more clearly, not just look trustworthy. Next, the **reasoning loop** — try, check, retry — like fitting jigsaw pieces or Maya tasting and fixing her cake. That looping is what makes AI far stronger on genuinely hard problems, even though it costs more time.

Finally, we met the **reasoning models** of 2026: built to pause and think, often with a "think harder" mode you can switch on when a problem deserves it. And our detective reminded us of the heart of it all: the breakthrough wasn't a bigger brain, it was the patience to slow down.

You can now explain reasoning AI to anyone. Tomorrow we add the next human-like power: a memory.
slides.speakerNotes: Recap by retracing the six ideas in order. End warmly and point forward to memory (Day 17).
slides.marketConnection: Knowing when to flip on 'reasoning' mode is fast becoming an everyday 2026 skill, at work and at home.

keyTakeaways: A quick blurted answer works for easy questions, but hard problems need step-by-step working-out
Chain of thought means the AI shows its work, which helps it think better, not just look trustworthy
The reasoning loop is try, check, retry — the AI treats its first answer as a draft, not the final word
Looping costs time but makes AI far more reliable on tricky math, logic, planning, and coding
Reasoning models in 2026 often have a 'think harder' mode you switch on when a problem deserves it
The breakthrough wasn't a bigger brain — it was giving AI permission to slow down and check itself
marketSnapshot2026: By 2026, 'reasoning' or 'extended thinking' modes are a standard feature across major AI assistants. People increasingly toggle them on for hard tasks — research, analysis, complex coding, big decisions — and leave them off for quick everyday questions. These slower, step-by-step answers are typically a bit costlier and take longer, so a genuinely useful new skill has emerged: knowing when to ask the AI to think hard versus when a fast reply is plenty. Tools that visibly show their reasoning, with an expandable 'thinking' panel, have become common and are valued for being easier to trust and double-check.
homework: Find an AI tool you use that has a 'think harder', 'reasoning', or 'extended thinking' option. Ask it one tricky multi-step question (for example, a small logic puzzle or a word problem with a twist) in the normal fast mode, then ask the exact same question with the reasoning mode on. Notice two things: did the slower answer change, and did the AI show any of its working steps? Jot down one sentence on which answer you trusted more and why.
nextDayTeaser: Tomorrow, on Day 17 — 'Giving AI a Memory' — we tackle a frustrating quirk: most AI forgets you the moment the chat ends, like a brilliant colleague with amnesia. We'll explore how AI is learning to remember who you are, what you like, and what you talked about last time — and why that changes everything about working with it.
